name: Phosphorus
name_origin: From the Greek 'phosphoros' meaning 'light-bearer', referring to the chemiluminescent glow of white phosphorus in air.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Phosphorus is the eleventh most abundant element in Earth's crust (~0.1%). It does not occur as a free element in nature because of its high reactivity; it is found in phosphate rocks (apatites) and in all living tissues.
states: DNA, RNA, ATP, phospholipids, and bones in living organisms

compounds.name: Phosphoric acid
compounds.summary: A triprotic acid used in fertilizers, food additives (acidulant in colas), and rust removal.

compounds.name: Phosphorus pentoxide (P4O10)
compounds.summary: A potent dehydrating agent and the anhydride of phosphoric acid.

compounds.name: Phosphine
compounds.summary: A toxic, flammable gas used as a fumigant and as a doping agent in semiconductors.

compounds.name: Phosphorus trichloride
compounds.summary: An important industrial intermediate for making pesticides, plasticizers, and other phosphorus compounds.

compounds.name: Hydroxyapatite
compounds.summary: The principal mineral component of bones and tooth enamel.

compounds.name: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP)
compounds.summary: The universal energy-carrier molecule of living cells.

uses: Phosphate fertilizers for agriculture (largest single use globally)
Detergents and water softeners (as polyphosphates, though use has declined)
Match heads and pyrotechnic compositions (red phosphorus on safety match strikers)
Steel alloying to improve strength and corrosion resistance
Food additives and beverages (phosphoric acid in colas, leavening agents)
reactivity_summary: Phosphorus exists in several allotropes with very different properties: white phosphorus is highly reactive, pyrophoric, and toxic, while red and black phosphorus are more stable. White phosphorus ignites spontaneously in air above ~30 degrees Celsius and reacts vigorously with oxygen and halogens.
isotopes_summary: Phosphorus has only one stable isotope, P-31. P-32 (half-life 14.3 days) and P-33 (half-life 25.3 days) are widely used as radioactive tracers in biochemistry and medicine.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Phosphorus
